title: Meet Your Brain
brainConcept: Neuroplasticity continues through adolescence; the prefrontal cortex finishes wiring in the mid-20s while reward circuitry peaks now.
objective: I can explain how my brain's current wiring shapes my reactions and why deliberate practice changes it.

flow.connect: Welcome to Brain Lab. Opening prompt: name one habit or skill you've genuinely changed in yourself, and what it took.
flow.teach: Your brain is still under construction in a useful way: highly plastic, with a fast reward/emotion system and a prefrontal cortex (judgment, planning, impulse control) that keeps maturing into your mid-20s. This isn't an excuse — it's leverage. Skills you deliberately practice now wire in deeply.
flow.practice: Frame the program as a self-experiment: each person names one outcome (less stress, better focus, fewer blow-ups) they want to test over six weeks. Brief retrieval demo to preview how we'll make sessions stick.
flow.close: Each person states their six-week self-experiment goal in one sentence.

contentQuestions.text: How does your brain's current wiring explain strong reactions or impulsivity?
Why is 'still developing' an advantage, not just a limitation?
What would it look like to treat this as a self-experiment?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: which brain system is still maturing into your mid-20s?
Where this week did your reward system outrun your judgment?
What's the first measurable sign your experiment is working?

title: Spot Your Signals
brainConcept: Chronic stress keeps the threat system activated; recognizing early signals prevents escalation and protects working memory.
objective: I can map my personal stress and emotion signals and the thresholds where I usually notice them.

flow.connect: Echo: one line on why practice rewires the brain. Then a brief attention check (single-point focus; notice the drift).
flow.teach: Under chronic stress, the threat system stays partly switched on, draining attention and working memory you need for school. Self-management starts with detection: body signals, thought patterns (catastrophizing, tunnel vision), and behavior signals (snapping, avoiding, scrolling).
flow.practice: Each person maps their top signals across three categories (body / thought / behavior) for stress and for anger, and marks the intensity where they currently catch each.
flow.close: Each person names the signal they want to detect earlier and why it matters.

contentQuestions.text: How does ongoing stress affect your attention and memory?
What's a thought-pattern signal you notice when overwhelmed?
Why is detecting a signal at a 3 more useful than at an 8?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: name the three categories of signals.
When did you catch a signal early this week?
Which signal still sneaks past you?

title: Catch the Wave
brainConcept: Affect labeling and controlled breathing down-regulate the amygdala and restore prefrontal control.
objective: I can apply a regulation strategy to bring myself back to baseline during a real stressor.

flow.connect: Echo: each person names an early signal. Then one round of paced breathing to anchor.
flow.teach: When the amygdala spikes, judgment narrows. Two evidence-based resets: name the emotion precisely (affect labeling lowers amygdala activity) and lengthen your exhale (activates the calming branch of the nervous system). Precision matters — 'I'm overwhelmed and behind' beats 'I'm fine.'
flow.practice: Teach the physiological sigh; practice two rounds. Then build a personal 90-second reset routine (label + breath + a grounding action). Pair-share where it fits in their day.
flow.close: Each person commits to one reset and the specific trigger they'll attach it to.

contentQuestions.text: Why does precise labeling work better than vague labeling?
How does a long exhale physically calm you down?
Where in your week would a 90-second reset matter most?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: name the two evidence-based resets.
Describe a stressor you reset this week.
What trigger still gets past your reset?

title: Aim Your Spotlight
brainConcept: Attention is finite and switching is costly; environment design and retrieval-based study beat passive review.
objective: I can design my study setup and use a retrieval strategy to learn more in less time.

flow.connect: Echo: each person shares a reset routine. Then a 45-second focus sprint with a planted distraction.
flow.teach: Task-switching taxes working memory; phones exploit dopamine to hijack the spotlight. Two upgrades: design the environment (phone in another room, single task, time-block) and study by retrieval — quiz yourself instead of re-reading. Re-reading feels productive but builds weak memory; recall builds strong memory.
flow.practice: Each person redesigns one real study block (environment + a retrieval method like self-quizzing or the blurting technique). Share and refine with a partner.
flow.close: Each person commits to one environment change and one retrieval method for this week.

contentQuestions.text: Why does re-reading feel effective but work poorly?
What makes retrieval practice build stronger memory?
Which environment change would most improve your focus?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: retrieval vs. re-reading — which wins, and why?
How did your redesigned study block go?
What's your hardest distraction to design out?

title: Read the Room
brainConcept: The brain infers others' states from sparse cues and fills gaps with bias; context and checking reduce error.
objective: I can read social and contextual cues accurately and resist jumping to conclusions.

flow.connect: Echo: each person shares a study upgrade. Then a quick tone demo (one word, three meanings).
flow.teach: Social cognition runs on incomplete data; your brain fills gaps with assumptions shaped by mood and bias — and digital communication strips out tone entirely. Strong social readers separate observation ('they were quiet') from interpretation ('they're mad at me') and verify before reacting.
flow.practice: Ambiguous digital scenario (short, cold reply). Generate multiple plausible interpretations, identify what extra info would clarify, and draft a low-key check-in.
flow.close: Each person names an interpretation they tend to default to and a way to verify it.

contentQuestions.text: How do mood and bias distort the way you read people?
Why is digital communication especially easy to misread?
What's the difference between an observation and an interpretation?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: observation vs. interpretation — define each.
When did you verify before reacting this week?
What's your most common false assumption about others?

title: Walk in Their Shoes
brainConcept: Cognitive perspective-taking and empathy are distinct skills; both can be strengthened with practice.
objective: I can take another's perspective on a charged issue without losing my own.

flow.connect: Echo: each person shares an assumption they verified. Then a 'steelman' warm-up (state the other side's best case).
flow.teach: There's understanding what someone thinks (cognitive perspective-taking) and feeling with them (empathy) — different circuits, both trainable. You build the model by connecting to your own analogous experiences (Velcro). Steelmanning — stating the other side's strongest version — sharpens it and lowers conflict.
flow.practice: Take a relevant, charged disagreement. Each person genuinely steelmans the opposing view and links it to a personal experience, then restates their own view with that understanding intact.
flow.close: Each person finishes: 'Their strongest point is ___, and I still think ___.'

contentQuestions.text: How is understanding someone different from feeling with them?
Why does steelmanning reduce conflict?
Can you hold your own view and theirs at the same time? How?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: cognitive perspective-taking vs. empathy — the difference?
Describe a time you steelmanned someone this week.
Which views are hardest for you to steelman?

title: Say It So They Hear It
brainConcept: Defensiveness is a threat response; framing around shared interests keeps the listener's prefrontal cortex engaged (Velcro).
objective: I can advocate for my needs and set a boundary without escalating.

flow.connect: Echo: each person shares a view they steelmanned. Then 'turn blame into a request' warm-up.
flow.teach: Blame and 'always/never' trigger a threat response and end real listening. Assertive communication = clear + respectful: 'I feel ___ when ___ because ___; what I need is ___.' Boundaries are a form of this. Frame around shared interests so their thinking brain stays online.
flow.practice: Higher-stakes scenario (a friend pressuring you, or a family curfew conflict). Draft and rehearse an assertive ask or boundary; partner coaches on tone and clarity.
flow.close: Each person shares one assertive statement or boundary for a real situation.

contentQuestions.text: Why does defensiveness shut down a conversation?
How is a boundary different from a demand or an ultimatum?
How does framing around shared interests help your message land?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what's the assertive statement frame?
Did you set a boundary or make an assertive ask this week?
What conversation are you avoiding that this could help?

title: Fix-It Talk
brainConcept: Rehearsed repair scripts are retrievable under stress; accountability accelerates de-escalation (Echo).
objective: I can repair a significant conflict by taking accountability and co-creating a solution.

flow.connect: Echo: each person recalls the assertive frame. Then a brief cool-down reset.
flow.teach: Mature repair prioritizes the relationship over being right. Steps: regulate first, exchange perspectives without interrupting, take genuine accountability for your part, and co-create a concrete fix. Accountability is the fastest de-escalator because it disarms the other person's defenses.
flow.practice: Role-play a real-type rupture (a betrayed confidence, a public embarrassment). Run the full sequence; debrief which step felt hardest and why.
flow.close: Each person names the step they most need to strengthen.

contentQuestions.text: Why does genuine accountability de-escalate so quickly?
What separates a real fix from a fake 'sorry'?
When is repair worth it even if you were 'right'?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: list the repair steps.
Describe a conflict you repaired this week.
Which step is hardest for you, and why?

title: Stop & Plan
brainConcept: Pre-commitment and a pause counteract in-the-moment reward bias and social influence.
objective: I can use pre-commitment and a decision process to make choices aligned with my goals.

flow.connect: Echo: each person names a repair step. Then a 'pre-decide' warm-up: state a rule you set for yourself in advance.
flow.teach: In the moment, reward bias and peer presence push fast choices. Two counter-moves: pre-commitment (decide your line before you're in the situation) and a pause to run a decision process — clarify the real goal, generate options, project outcomes, check values, act. Pre-deciding outsources willpower to your calmer past self.
flow.practice: Real-stakes scenario (party, academic shortcut, risky group dare). Each person pre-commits a rule and scripts how they'll hold it; group pressure-tests it.
flow.close: Each person states one pre-commitment they'll hold this week.

contentQuestions.text: Why is deciding in advance more reliable than in-the-moment willpower?
How do peers and reward bias distort fast decisions?
What makes a pre-commitment realistic to keep?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what is pre-commitment?
Did a pre-commitment hold up this week?
What upcoming situation needs one?

title: Weigh It Out
brainConcept: Good decisions integrate long-term outcomes, ethics, and impact on others — not just immediate payoff.
objective: I can evaluate a complex decision across time, ethics, and impact on others.

flow.connect: Echo: each person shares a pre-commitment. Then a 'fast-forward one year' warm-up on a current choice.
flow.teach: Hard choices rarely have a clean answer. A solid process weighs outcomes across time (now vs. future self), checks ethics (is it honest, fair, legal), and considers impact on others — then aligns with your values. This deliberate simulation is what the reward system skips.
flow.practice: Genuinely ambiguous dilemma (reporting a friend's risky behavior, a costly honesty call). Build a decision matrix; identify the values in tension and defend a choice.
flow.close: Each person finishes: 'Weighing it fully, I'd ___ because ___.'

contentQuestions.text: How do you weigh your future self against your present self?
What ethical checks belong in a hard decision?
How do you decide when two values are in tension?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: name the three dimensions we weigh decisions across.
Describe a complex choice you weighed this week.
Which value tension is hardest for you?

title: Put It Together
brainConcept: Expertise is an integrated schema run with low effort; sequencing skills under load is the real test.
objective: I can integrate and sequence Brain Lab skills to handle a high-stakes real-world situation.

flow.connect: Rapid Echo: the group lists every tool and its purpose in 60 seconds.
flow.teach: Real competence isn't knowing the steps — it's running the connected web under pressure. The reliable order: regulate, then read the situation, then communicate, then decide. Skipping regulation sinks everything downstream.
flow.practice: Complex layered scenario (conflict + audience + time pressure, e.g., a heated group project blowup before a deadline). Map and perform the full sequence; critique alternatives.
flow.close: Each person names their strongest and weakest link in the chain.

contentQuestions.text: Why does the sequence (regulate first) matter so much under pressure?
Which tools does the layered scenario demand, and in what order?
Where does your chain tend to break?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: name the four-stage order for handling a hard moment.
Describe a real situation where you ran the sequence this week.
Which link will you strengthen, and how?

title: Rewire & Reflect
brainConcept: Durable change comes from spaced retrieval and identity-based habits, not motivation alone.
objective: I can build a maintenance plan that turns a skill into part of how I see myself.

flow.connect: Echo lap: circle naming each tool and a real situation it fits until exhausted.
flow.teach: Six weeks of spaced practice and retrieval is exactly how the brain consolidates durable skills. Motivation fades; what lasts is identity ('I'm someone who pauses before reacting') plus cues and repetition. Revisit your week-one self-experiment and judge the results.
flow.practice: Each person writes a maintenance plan: target skill, identity statement, trigger cue, and a tracking method — then reviews their six-week experiment outcome with a partner.
flow.close: Each person names one concrete way they've changed and one skill they'll carry forward.

contentQuestions.text: Why does identity-based habit-building outlast motivation?
What were the results of your six-week self-experiment?
What cue and tracking method will keep your skill alive?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what makes a habit durable beyond motivation?
How are you different from session one?
Which skill will you carry into next year?
